skill.name: share-skill
skill.description: Share local Claude skills by migrating them into git repositories, creating symlinks, configuring remotes, and generating documentation.
skill.summary: Migrate local Claude skills into version-controlled repositories.
skill.author: guo-yu

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified extensive shell, filesystem, and network behavior, but many individual hits are Markdown examples or translated copies rather than standalone executable code. The skill intent is legitimate git-based sharing, yet it asks for broad Claude Bash permissions, moves files under ~/.claude, creates symlinks, invokes other skills, and can push local skill contents to git remotes, so it should not be published without human review and explicit user warnings.

high_findings.title: Broad Claude Bash Permission Expansion
high_findings.description: The allow workflow instructs users to add wide Bash permissions for reading ~/.claude files, finding files under ~/.claude, moving skills, creating symlinks, and running any git command. This is intended functionality, but it greatly increases blast radius if a malicious skill name, remote, or later instruction is introduced.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission list is explicit and grants broad command patterns. The risk is semantic rather than accidental because the skill asks users to persist these permissions in Claude Code settings.

high_findings.title: Local Skill Directory Move And Symlink Replacement
high_findings.description: The migration flow moves a skill from ~/.claude/skills into a code repository and replaces the original path with a symlink. This can alter local assistant behavior and could move unintended directories if skill name handling is weak.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file move and symlink operations are clearly documented. The skill includes some overwrite checks, but the operation still modifies core local skill storage.

high_findings.title: Potential Publication Of Private Local Skill Content
high_findings.description: The workflow can add a git remote and push migrated skill content, then later commit and push documentation and translations. This can expose private prompts, local notes, or proprietary skill content if users do not review the target remote and files first.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The git push commands and remote configuration flow are explicit. This is legitimate for sharing, but it is a high-impact data exposure path.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Invocation Of Other Installed Skills
medium_findings.description: The post-migration flow can invoke skill-i18n with no prompt and overwrite options, and documentation generation can call a selected UI skill. This creates a supply-chain dependency on locally installed skills outside this package.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external skill calls are documented and appear optional. Risk depends on which local skills are installed, so confidence is high for the pattern but lower for exploitability.

medium_findings.title: Generated Documentation Uses Configurable Domain And File Writes
medium_findings.description: The docs workflow writes or removes docs/CNAME based on configuration and updates files under the generated docs directory. This is expected for GitHub Pages, but it can publish links to a user-controlled domain and alter repository content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is visible and scoped to docs output. It is moderate risk because a misleading custom domain or unreviewed generated files could be committed and pushed.

medium_findings.title: Verification Script Reads User Home Configuration
medium_findings.description: The verification script checks for ~/.claude/share-skill-config.json and reads fields from it. It does not exfiltrate the data, but it touches home-directory configuration outside the repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script path and config access are explicit. I did not find evidence of network transmission from the script, so the concern is local exposure and scope, not exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are Mostly Cache-Busting Examples
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts include Markdown examples using md5 to create short cache-busting strings for docs assets. This is not used for password storage, signing, or security-sensitive cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding section states the purpose is browser cache versioning. That context makes the weak-crypto finding a low-risk false positive.

low_findings.title: C2 Keyword Static Hits Are Documentation Style Text
low_findings.description: The C2 keyword alerts align with design preset language, colors, and generated site examples rather than command-and-control behavior. I did not find evidence of malware callback logic in the reviewed lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context is documentation about visual styles and output text. The static keyword match is weak, but broader network behavior still exists through git pushes.

low_findings.title: Translated Markdown Duplicates Inflate Static Counts
low_findings.description: Many static findings are repeated in SKILL.ja.md and SKILL.zh-CN.md because they are translations of the same workflow. The duplicates increase the count but do not add separate behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The translated files mirror the command table and workflow sections. They matter for user-facing instructions, but they are not independent executable implementations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wildcard Bash Allow Rules
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends persisted wildcard permissions such as Bash(git *) and Bash(cat ~/.claude/*). Wildcards reduce future confirmation barriers for sensitive filesystem and git operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wildcard permission patterns are present in the documented settings block. They directly match the static filesystem and external command risk categories.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unreviewed Remote Push Flow
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented flow can resolve a remote, add it as origin, and push automatically. A wrong or attacker-controlled remote could receive local skill content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The remote add and push commands are explicit. The skill asks for confirmation in some cases, but the publication path remains sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Home Directory Skill Discovery
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill searches under ~/.claude for SKILL.md files and skill directories. This is needed for discovery, but it enumerates local assistant configuration and custom skills.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The discovery commands are documented and scoped to ~/.claude. This confirms the filesystem enumeration pattern without showing separate exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Local skills are hard to version, review, and publish safely. This skill guides migration into git repositories with symlinks, remotes, documentation, and repeatable sharing steps.
actual_capabilities: Find local skills under Claude skill directories.
Move a selected skill into a configured code repository.
Create symlinks so Claude can still load migrated skills.
Initialize git commits and configure repository remotes.
Generate README and documentation site content for shared skills.
Configure aliases, documentation style, and custom domain settings.
limitations: Requires git, jq, and compatible shell tools for many workflows.
Broad filesystem and git permissions require careful user review.
Remote pushes can publish private skill content if used incorrectly.
Generated documentation quality depends on source skill metadata.

use_cases.title: Publish a Personal Skill
use_cases.description: Move a local skill into a git repository and prepare it for public sharing.

use_cases.title: Organize Team Skills
use_cases.description: Collect shared skills in one repository with consistent README and documentation updates.

use_cases.title: Maintain Skill Documentation
use_cases.description: Generate documentation pages and language files for a growing skills repository.

prompt_templates.title: List Local Skills
prompt_templates.prompt: List my local skills that can be migrated, and do not move anything yet.

prompt_templates.title: Share One Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Migrate my port-allocator skill into my default skills repository, then show me the files before any push.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Remote Alias
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure a github remote alias for git@github.com:my-org/skills.git and confirm the resolved target.

prompt_templates.title: Generate And Publish Docs
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate documentation for my skills repository, update READMEs, and ask before committing or pushing changes.

output_examples.input: List my local skills that can be migrated.
output: Shows available skills found under the local Claude skills directory.
Separates skills already migrated through symlinks.
Does not change files during listing.

output_examples.input: Migrate my port-allocator skill and prepare a remote.
output: Reports the source skill path and target repository path.
Creates the repository copy, symlink, initial commit, and remote configuration.
Asks for confirmation before publishing when remote behavior is unclear.

output_examples.input: Generate documentation for the skills repository.
output: Creates documentation files, README updates, and optional language versions.
Applies the selected documentation style and custom domain setting.
Summarizes changed files before commit or push.

best_practices: Review every file diff before committing or pushing migrated skills.
Use explicit git remote URLs for non-default repositories.
Grant permissions only for the session and scope you understand.
anti_patterns: Running the allow workflow without reading the Bash permissions.
Pushing a migrated skill before checking for private prompts or secrets.
Using vague natural language for repository targets or custom domains.

faq.question: Does this skill execute shell commands?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow documents git, file move, symlink, and discovery commands that need user review.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish without review?
faq.answer: No. The audit marks it high risk because it can modify local skill storage and push content remotely.

faq.question: Where does the skill store configuration?
faq.answer: It uses ~/.claude/share-skill-config.json for repository, remote, and documentation settings.

faq.question: Can it publish private information?
faq.answer: Yes, if a migrated skill contains private content and the user pushes it to a remote repository.

faq.question: Does it support documentation generation?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes README updates, GitHub Pages documentation, styles, translations, and custom domains.

faq.question: Should teams use this directly?
faq.answer: Teams should review the permission model, require code review, and use approved remotes only.
